output_text:
Summary: Critique of Section 4 with specific concerns about a likely error in the Theorem 2 inequality direction, plus notation, hedging, and quantitative claims to fix.

## Substantive concerns

**1. The Dobrushin bound likely goes the wrong way.** This is the most important issue.

The Dobrushin ergodicity coefficient τ(M) satisfies τ(M) ≥ |λ₂(M)| for row-stochastic M (this is a standard inequality; equality is rare). Therefore

  1 / (1 − τ(M))  ≥  1 / (1 − λ₂(M)).

So replacing the spectral gap by the Dobrushin coefficient makes the prefactor *larger*, not smaller. You then claim a 1/√n factor rescues this. That needs to be justified explicitly — and the resulting "improvement" depends on a race between (1 − λ₂)/(1 − τ) (which is ≤ 1) and √n. For the worst-case M these can cancel; for benign M they don't. As written, Theorem 2 reads as an unconditional improvement, which I do not believe is correct. Either (a) state the inequality on κ in the correct direction, (b) restrict to a class of M where τ(M) − λ₂(M) is controlled, or (c) show me the line of the proof where the √n enters — I suspect it is doing more work than the text admits.

**2. The √n in a condition-number bound is unusual and should be motivated in-text.** Readers will expect condition numbers of (I − M)|_{1^⊥} to be dimension-independent or to grow with n. A *decrease* with √n is a strong claim. One sentence pointing to the mechanism (mixing in ℓ¹ vs. ℓ², equivalence-of-norm constants on the simplex, an averaging argument over coordinates?) would defuse skepticism.

**3. "Order-of-magnitude tightening" overstates the numbers.** √50 ≈ 7, √400 = 20. Call it "a factor of 7–20×" or "nearly an order of magnitude at the upper end." Reviewers will check.

**4. The Wilkinson model is for a single matvec; the iteration accumulates.** "Each matrix-vector product is computed with relative error bounded by nη" is the per-step bound. The fixed-point error after k steps is the *accumulated* effect of these errors filtered through (I − M)|_{1^⊥}. State this explicitly. The current phrasing — "once the spectral component has decayed" — is doing too much hand-waving for an analysis whose contribution is rigor. Quantify: for k ≥ k* := ⌈log(ε)/log(λ₂/λ₁)⌉ the spectral residual is dominated by the rounding floor, etc.

**5. Norm consistency.** The iteration is normalized in ℓ¹ but the error bound uses an unsubscripted ‖·‖. Pick one and stay consistent, or note explicitly where you switch and why the simplex geometry makes the switch harmless (factor of √n between ℓ¹ and ℓ² on R^n — which, incidentally, is suspiciously the same √n appearing in Theorem 2; double-check you have not absorbed the norm-equivalence constant twice).

## Notation / exposition

**6. Define ‖·‖_{1^⊥}.** It is not standard. Presumably the operator norm restricted to the orthogonal complement of the all-ones vector — but readers should not have to guess.

**7. (I − M)^+ vs. (I − M)|_{1^⊥}.** You use both. The pseudoinverse and the inverse on the invariant subspace agree here, but a one-line remark clarifying this avoids confusion.

**8. "kappa((I − M)|_{1^⊥})"** — condition number in which norm? The bound 1/(1 − λ₂) is the 2-norm condition number under symmetry; for general row-stochastic M it is the spectral gap of the normalized form, which is not the same thing. State the norm.

**9. "n × η" — define η once at first use** (you do call it unit roundoff, good; just put it in parentheses on first appearance: "η (unit roundoff)").

## Framing

**10. The closing paragraph is good** — the "complement, not replace" framing is the right move. Keep it. But move the WiC-Amb sentence into its own (sub)section or at least give a forward-reference: "(Section 5)" or "(Appendix B)" so readers know where the numerics live.

**11. "Has not, to our knowledge, been treated rigorously"** — fine, but you should cite the closest prior attempt and say why it falls short (one sentence). Otherwise reviewers assume you didn't look.

**12. Park et al.** appears with no year and no first citation context. Either this is the first reference (give full citation) or you assume the reader has seen it earlier in the paper — make sure that is true.

## Suggested order of operations

1. Resolve concern #1. If Theorem 2 is wrong as stated, the rest of the revisions are wasted.
2. Once #1 is settled, rewrite the second paragraph to state the regime of validity explicitly (#4) and fix the norm bookkeeping (#5).
3. Cosmetic fixes (#3, #6–#12) at the end.

## Uncertainty disclosure

The direction of the τ(M) vs. λ₂(M) inequality is standard (Seneta, *Non-negative Matrices and Markov Chains*, Ch. 4), and I am confident concern #1 is real as the text currently reads — but I have not seen your Theorem 2 proof, and it is possible the √n factor genuinely dominates the (1 − λ₂)/(1 − τ) loss for the matrix class you have in mind. The norm-equivalence coincidence in #5 is a hunch worth checking, not a confirmed double-count. The numerical claim in #3 is arithmetic and is correct.
